sections.title: Use
items: A subject pronoun tells us who or what performs the action of the verb.

sections.title: Example
items: Look at those children! **They** are playing football.
Maria loves baking, and **she** makes the best cakes in town.

sections.title: Use
items: As the object of a verb (receives the action): The teacher called **him** to the front.
After prepositions: Could you send the documents to **us** by Friday?

sections.title: Use
items: To indicate who something belongs to. They always come before a noun: I left **my** keys on the table.

sections.title: Be careful
items: The cat licked **its** paw. (= the cat's paw — possessive determiner)
**It's** a lovely day today. (= It is ... — contraction)

sections.title: Use
items: To talk about ownership without repeating the noun: I forgot my umbrella. Can I borrow **yours**?

sections.title: Be careful
items: There is no possessive pronoun for **it**.

sections.title: Use
items: When the subject and the object of the verb are the same person or thing: She hurt **herself** while cycling.
To add emphasis — to stress that someone did something without any help: We painted the whole house **ourselves**!

exercises.title: A: Subject pronouns
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with a subject pronoun.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with a subject pronoun.

items.sentence: Sofia and I are hungry. ...... want some lunch.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is sofia and I = first person plural, so the correct answer is "We".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Where is Leo? ...... is upstairs.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is leo = male singular, so the correct answer is "He".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: My tablet is old. ...... doesn't work very well.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is my tablet = thing, so the correct answer is "It".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Hugo and Clara are cousins. ...... live in Valencia.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is hugo and Clara = they, so the correct answer is "They".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "They" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Hugo and Clara are cousins. ...... live in Valencia.".

items.sentence: I'm not ready, but ...... can leave in five minutes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is speaker talking about themselves, so the correct answer is "I".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Mia, are ...... coming with us?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is direct question to Mia, so the correct answer is "you".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The plants are dry. Please water ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is the plants = plural things, so the correct answer is "them".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: My cousins are visiting, so I'm showing ...... the city.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is my cousins = plural, so the correct answer is "them".

items.sentence: Please tell ...... the answer when you know it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is object of tell, so the correct answer is "us".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "us" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Please tell ...... the answer when you know it.".

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "my" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "my" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "mine" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.mine: "mine" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("coat?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.mine: "mine" doesn't fit here → "my" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("coat?").

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "her" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "her" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "hers" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.hers: "hers" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("phone.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.hers: "hers" doesn't fit here → "her" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("phone.").

exercises.title: D: Possessive pronouns
exercises.instructions: Complete with the correct possessive pronoun.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete with the correct possessive pronoun.

items.sentence: This pen belongs to me. It's ...... .

explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: G: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one pronoun mistake. Write the correct word.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one pronoun mistake. Write the correct word.

items.sentence: This is theirs house.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.theirs: "theirs" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "their".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "theirs" — the rule here needs "their" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: This is <mistake>theirs</mistake> house.

items.sentence: Him is my cousin.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.Him: "Him" is the highlighted error — use "He" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "Him", not elsewhere — replace it with "He" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: <mistake>Him</mistake> is my cousin.

items.sentence: Can you explain this to we?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.we: "we" is the highlighted error — use "us" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "we", not elsewhere — replace it with "us" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: Can you explain this to <mistake>we</mistake>?
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Can you explain this to us?
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Can you explain this to us?

items.sentence: The boys repaired the bike by himself.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.

wrongOptions.himself: "himself" is the highlighted error — use "themselves" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "himself", not elsewhere — replace it with "themselves" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: The boys repaired the bike by <mistake>himself</mistake>.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: The boys repaired the bike by themselves.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The boys repaired the bike by themselves.

items.sentence: The dog is sleeping in it's basket.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.it's: "it's" is the highlighted error — use "its" here instead.
wrongOptions.it: "it" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "its".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "it's" — the rule here needs "its" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: The dog is sleeping in <mistake>it's</mistake> basket.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: The dog is sleeping in its basket.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The dog is sleeping in its basket.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

exercises.title: H: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one pronoun mistake. Find the error and write the whole sentence correctly.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one pronoun mistake. Find the error and write the whole sentence correctly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: After is with no noun.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail — choose who/which/that/whose to match the noun they describe.

wrongOptions.After: Check how "After" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.is: Check how "is" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: That umbrella is <mistake>her.</mistake>

items.answer: Leo fixed the computer by himself.
acceptedAnswers: Leo fixed the computer by himself.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: By + reflexive.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Leo fixed the computer by himself.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: Leo fixed the computer by himself.

wrongOptions.By: Check how "By" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Leo fixed the computer by <mistake>him.</mistake>
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Leo fixed the computer by himself.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Before classroom.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Time clauses set the order of events — match verb tenses to show which action happened first.

wrongOptions.Before: Check how "Before" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.classroom: Check how "classroom" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: This is <mistake>we</mistake> classroom.

items.answer: Could you show them the photos?
acceptedAnswers: Could you show them the photos?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Object of show.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Could you show them the photos?
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Object: Check how "Object" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.of: Check how "of" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Could you show <mistake>they</mistake> the photos?
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Could you show them the photos?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: After is with no noun.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Time clauses set the order of events — match verb tenses to show which action happened first.

wrongOptions.After: Check how "After" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.is: Check how "is" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: The blue bag is <mistake>she.</mistake>

explanationContent.whyCorrect: By + reflexive, plural.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: They prepared everything by themselves.

wrongOptions.By: Check how "By" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: They prepared everything by <mistake>them.</mistake>

exercises.title: I: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
Anna wrote the story without help from anyone.
Anna wrote the story ......

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is alone, without help, so the correct answer is "by herself".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "by herself", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The wallet belongs to David.
That is ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is belongs to David, so the correct answer is "David's wallet".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "David's wallet", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
My sister cut herself while cooking.
My sister ...... while cooking.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is hurt herself = injured herself, so the correct answer is "hurt herself".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "hurt herself", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
We cleaned the whole kitchen without any help.
We cleaned the whole kitchen ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no help, so the correct answer is "by ourselves".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "by ourselves", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Does the black backpack belong to you?
...... black backpack?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is question about ownership, so the correct answer is "Is that your".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "Is that your", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The students created the posters on their own.
The students created ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is they made the posters alone, so the correct answer is "the posters themselves".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the posters themselves", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
